title: The fidelity of controlled quantum teleportation in a noisy environment
authors: Wen-Jing Wei, Feng-Li Yan, Ting Gao
abstract: In this work, we investigate controlled quantum teleportation in the presence of noisy channels acting on the three-qubit resource state. We employ a series of generalized noisy channels that bridge the dephasing channels and amplitude damping channels while encompassing extensive intermediate scenarios. We provide an in-depth analysis of the degradation of the maximal average fidelity and the optimal average fidelity in controlled quantum teleportation induced by such noisy channels by deriving the analytical expression and examining several special cases. The analytical expression shows that attaining the optimal average fidelity requires Charlie's cooperation in performing a measurement at suitably chosen angles, and is also related to the initial state and the channel parameters. Our analysis reveals that the optimal average fidelity does not always decrease monotonically with the evolution parameter, instead, it first decreases and then increases. This non-monotonic behavior depends on the entanglement of the initial resource state, as well as on the parameters of the channel traversed by the first qubit.
summary: This work analyzes how various types of noise affect the success rate (fidelity) of controlled quantum teleportation. By deriving complex analytical expressions, the authors show that fidelity does not always decrease monotonically with increasing noise; instead, it can exhibit non-monotonic behavior. This highlights that the impact of decoherence is highly dependent on both the initial entanglement and which qubit interacts with the noisy channel.
main_problem: The study investigates how noise degrades the fidelity of controlled quantum teleportation protocols.
main_result: The optimal average fidelity can exhibit non-monotonic behavior, first decreasing and then increasing with the evolution parameter, depending on initial entanglement and channel parameters.
method: The authors derive analytical expressions for the maximal average fidelity ($F_{	ext{max}}$) by modeling noise using generalized noisy channels parameterized by Kraus operators.
model_or_system: The system involves controlled quantum teleportation utilizing a tripartite entangled resource state $|\Psiangle_{ABC}$. Noise is modeled as local, independent quantum channels ($\Lambda_{ABC} = \Lambda_A \otimes \Lambda_B \otimes \Lambda_C$) acting on the three qubits.
key_observables: Maximal average fidelity ($F_{	ext{max}}$) and optimal average fidelity.
important_parameters: Channel parameters ($q, k, r$), evolution angles ($\zeta_A, \zeta_B, \zeta_C$), and initial state entanglement parameters ($a_0, a_1$).
main_assumptions: The noise is assumed to be local and independent across the three qubits. The analysis often simplifies by considering only one or two qubits passing through noise.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate $F_{	ext{max}}$ as functions of channel parameters ($\zeta_A, q$) for different initial state conditions ($a_1$ vs $a_0$), visually confirming the non-monotonic dependence on noise strength.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first defining the noisy resource state and calculating $F_{	ext{max}}$ generally. It then analyzes specific cases (e.g., only one qubit noisy, all three qubits noisy) to derive simplified analytical expressions and demonstrate the non-monotonic behavior based on parameter regimes.
why_interesting: The finding that fidelity can increase after an initial drop due to noise is crucial for understanding practical quantum communication channels, suggesting potential recovery or resilience mechanisms beyond simple decay models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
